caveats: a: The occupation source covers bakeries and tortillas together and is from 2023.
a: The automation source is an equipment vendor's configurable line, not an adoption census or independently verified labor study.
a: Already automated tasks are excluded, so highly automated plants have less remaining exposure than greenfield equipment capability suggests.
rho: No representative US LMM tortilla automation-adoption survey was found.
rho: Corn and flour tortilla processes have different forming, cooking, moisture, and shelf-life constraints.
rho: Implementation assumes access to competent maintenance and controls talent.
e: The public channel evidence comes from GRUMA, a much larger company than the lens firms.
e: NAICS 311830 does not disclose customer channel or contract-manufacturing share at firm level.
e: The supplied n is margin-bridged and may include businesses outside the intended EBITDA band or service model.
s5: The succession survey is cross-industry and measures intentions rather than completed control transfers.
s5: Manufacturing age evidence combines manufacturing with mining and utilities.
s5: No tortilla-specific closed-deal denominator was found.
q: GRUMA combines corn flour, tortillas, and related products and has national brands and scale advantages.
q: The own-label competition study is UK-based and not tortilla-specific.
q: Foodservice, private-label retail, and industrial accounts can have materially different repricing cadence.
d5: GRUMA figures combine tortillas, corn flour, and other products and represent a single large supplier.
d5: Demographic growth does not translate one-for-one into tortilla demand, and mainstream consumers also matter.
d5: Low-carbohydrate preferences, restaurant traffic, package downsizing, and customer insourcing can reduce domestic outsourced volume.
o: Large foodservice customers can internalize production or switch to centralized commissaries.
o: The construct concerns operator necessity, not remaining headcount.
o: Modified requirements may apply to some small facilities, though physical and food-safety accountability remains.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring per-case, per-pound, or per-shipment sales to foodservice operators, retailers, distributors, and industrial customers, often with customer specifications, route or distributor delivery, periodic price resets, and competitive account retention.
lens.deviation_from_default: The default lens is narrowed to manufacturers with material foodservice, industrial, retail private-label, distributor, or customer-specific supply because branded consumer products and direct retail activity are not coherently recurring outsourced services.
lens.excluded_activities: Primarily branded consumer-product manufacturers without a material outsourced-supply relationship, restaurant or retail tortillerias classified outside manufacturing, tortilla-chip manufacturers classified elsewhere, captive plants, shells, and firms outside the $1-10M normalized EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market US tortilla manufacturers that repeatedly supply external foodservice, industrial, retail private-label, distributor, or customer-specific accounts with corn or flour tortillas and related flatbreads classified in NAICS 311830.

a.bridge: BLS measures occupations for broader NAICS 311800, not tortilla manufacturing alone, and does not measure AI exposure. I map its production, packaging, inspection, material-moving, office, sales, and supervisory work to currently automatable tasks, informed by commercially available tortilla lines using vision, robots, automated weighing, environmental monitoring, and packaging. The bakery-heavy BLS mix may overstate hand baking and retail roles but understate the manual packaging and route complexity of smaller tortilla plants, so error direction is ambiguous.
caveats: The occupation source covers bakeries and tortillas together and is from 2023.
The automation source is an equipment vendor's configurable line, not an adoption census or independently verified labor study.
Already automated tasks are excluded, so highly automated plants have less remaining exposure than greenfield equipment capability suggests.
a.change_evidence: Six-digit wage-by-occupation payroll data and time-and-motion studies at at least ten LMM tortilla plants, including current automation status for inspection, stacking, packaging, scheduling, quality records, route planning, and order entry, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: Broader-industry employment is 49.70% production and 13.23% transportation/material moving, with packaging-machine operators at 6.71% and inspectors at 1.14%. Commercial tortilla systems demonstrate vision-guided handling, automatic stacking, sealing, weight inspection, monitoring, and robotic boxing. AI exposure therefore extends beyond office work into inspection and line decisions, but sanitation, maintenance, mixing, baking, handling, and delivery remain physical.

d5.bridge: GRUMA's US volume and channel commentary measures one large supplier's corn-flour, tortilla, and related-product business, not the exact outsourced basket, while Census demographics provide a demand-driver rather than tortilla volumes. I combine near-term foodservice weakness with strong better-for-you retail performance, overall population growth, and faster Hispanic population growth to estimate modest five-year real quantity growth. Brand share shifts and GRUMA's scale could make its trend diverge from LMM suppliers in either direction.
caveats: GRUMA figures combine tortillas, corn flour, and other products and represent a single large supplier.
Demographic growth does not translate one-for-one into tortilla demand, and mainstream consumers also matter.
Low-carbohydrate preferences, restaurant traffic, package downsizing, and customer insourcing can reduce domestic outsourced volume.
d5.change_evidence: Circana or NielsenIQ unit sales, Technomic foodservice tortilla usage, distributor case volumes, private-label share, and five-year Census shipment quantities for 311830, separated between corn and flour tortillas, would materially change the estimate.
d5.rationale: Tortillas serve retail, foodservice, and industrial markets and are supported by broad culinary adoption and demographic growth. The US Hispanic population grew 1.8% in 2023 and accounted for nearly 71% of national population growth. Against that, GRUMA USA volume fell 2% in 4Q24 because of price-sensitive foodservice, so the range allows near-flat demand as well as moderate growth.

caveats: The public channel evidence comes from GRUMA, a much larger company than the lens firms.
NAICS 311830 does not disclose customer channel or contract-manufacturing share at firm level.
The supplied n is margin-bridged and may include businesses outside the intended EBITDA band or service model.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census of the 114 dataset-estimated LMM candidates with revenue split by branded retail, private label, foodservice, industrial, distributor, and direct retail, plus captive status and customer-contract review, would materially move eligibility.
e.rationale: A leading US tortilla producer serves industrial, retail, and foodservice customers, confirming that recurring external supply channels are central to the industry. However, branded retail products, direct retail tortillerias, and customer-independent wholesale products do not all meet the outsourced-service lens. LMM firms are more likely than the largest national brands to rely on regional foodservice, distributor, private-label, and customer-specific accounts, but the qualifying revenue share is not published.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 311830 ($1,085,194,222) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 311830 ($6,316,155,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1525 (margins.json[31]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Food Processing EBITDA/Sales 15.25% (dominant subsector of NAICS 31)); revenue/employee $305,394 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 311830); class <5: 125 firms, avg revenue/firm $283,712 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $43,266 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 77 firms, avg revenue/firm $969,714 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $147,881 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 64 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,885,531 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $287,544 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 90 firms, avg revenue/firm $8,330,811 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,270,449 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 24 firms, avg revenue/firm $37,455,208 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $5,711,919 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 9 firms, avg revenue/firm $492,961,222 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $75,176,586 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 114

o.bridge: FDA requirements and physical line specifications establish continued facility and process accountability but do not directly measure operator-required demand. I infer that mixing, forming, cooking, cooling, inspection, packaging, sanitation, maintenance, and delivery cannot become a software-only service within five years. Customer self-production is possible and pulls the low case down, while automation changes labor content rather than eliminating the operator.
caveats: Large foodservice customers can internalize production or switch to centralized commissaries.
The construct concerns operator necessity, not remaining headcount.
Modified requirements may apply to some small facilities, though physical and food-safety accountability remains.
o.change_evidence: Customer insourcing surveys, announcements of restaurant or retailer commissary investment, evidence of autonomous food plants, and regulatory acceptance of remotely supervised preventive-control systems would materially change the range.
o.rationale: Tortillas are physical, perishable or shelf-life-managed foods made on equipment that must be cleaned, maintained, monitored, and controlled. Covered facilities require written hazard analysis, preventive controls, monitoring, corrective action, verification, records, sanitation, and allergen controls. Even highly automated lines therefore require an accountable manufacturing operator.

q.bridge: GRUMA's current US results show price sensitivity, deliberate profitability protection, and margin gains from efficiencies, but it is a scaled branded and foodservice operator. A UK competition study measures own-label food supplier bargaining across other categories. I translate those structures to LMM US tortilla account economics: operational benefits can be retained initially, while tenders, customer transparency, volume response, and renewals share them over five years. GRUMA's scale and brands likely overstate pricing power relative to lens firms.
caveats: GRUMA combines corn flour, tortillas, and related products and has national brands and scale advantages.
The own-label competition study is UK-based and not tortilla-specific.
Foodservice, private-label retail, and industrial accounts can have materially different repricing cadence.
q.change_evidence: Customer contracts, bid calendars, rebate and cost-down terms, account-level price-volume histories, gross-margin bridges, and renewal outcomes after measured productivity gains across LMM tortilla suppliers would materially change retention.
q.rationale: The US leader accepted a 2% quarterly volume decline, primarily in price-sensitive foodservice, while efficiencies reduced cost of sales and full-year EBITDA grew. That shows real ability to retain some operating benefit, but outsourced suppliers face strong account competition, price comparisons, retailer or distributor leverage, and periodic renegotiation. Retention should be meaningful but far from complete.

rho.bridge: Vendor specifications show technical feasibility, while FDA rules describe the validation, monitoring, sanitation, allergen, documentation, and training constraints faced by food plants. I translate those inputs into five-year implementation for LMM US tortilla manufacturers. Turnkey claims likely overstate brownfield ease and understate integration and workforce constraints, which pulls the base below the demonstrated technical ceiling.
caveats: No representative US LMM tortilla automation-adoption survey was found.
Corn and flour tortilla processes have different forming, cooking, moisture, and shelf-life constraints.
Implementation assumes access to competent maintenance and controls talent.
rho.change_evidence: Plant equipment-age inventories, OEE and scrap baselines, automation integrator quotations, FDA and customer audit histories, capex budgets, and measured pilots on live tortilla lines would materially change implementation expectations.
rho.rationale: Modular tortilla lines can integrate automatic forming, baking, cooling, stacking, vision-guided handling, weight inspection, packaging, boxing, and condition alerts. LMM plants can implement discrete inspection, planning, and packaging improvements within five years, but line downtime, capex, old layouts, recipe variability, sanitation, validation, customer audits, and limited engineering staff prevent full capture.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures stated sale or transfer plans for US employer-businesses across industries, and McKinsey reports older ownership and transfer friction for manufacturing broadly. I apply the 22% five-year employer intention rate as an upper anchor, reduce it for failed processes, closures, gifts without qualifying control, and internal reorganizations, then recognize above-average manufacturing owner age. The result may overstate completions if small tortilla firms are highly owner-dependent or understate them if family succession and strategic regional consolidation are unusually active.
caveats: The succession survey is cross-industry and measures intentions rather than completed control transfers.
Manufacturing age evidence combines manufacturing with mining and utilities.
No tortilla-specific closed-deal denominator was found.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, GF Data, BizBuySell, Axial, SBA 7(a), food-industry broker, and state business-registry data matched to 311830, plus owner-age and succession interviews with the LMM candidate list, would materially change this estimate.
s5.rationale: Gallup found 22% of employer-business owners planned a sale or transfer within five years, while over 60% of owners in manufacturing, mining, and utilities are over 55. Tortilla plants have transferable equipment, routes, customers, and recipes, yet customer concentration, food-safety diligence, owner relationships, working capital, and a thin small-deal buyer market constrain completed qualifying transfers.

principal_driver: A recurring staple-food supply relationship combines demographic demand support with practical inspection, packaging, planning, and back-office automation opportunities.
principal_weakness: Customer price sensitivity and tendering can dissipate savings, while the eligible firm and completed-transfer shares are only indirectly evidenced.

sources.supports: Defines NAICS 311830 as establishments primarily engaged in manufacturing tortillas and distinguishes related foods classified elsewhere.
sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 311830 Tortilla Manufacturing

sources.supports: Provides the broader bakeries-and-tortillas occupation mix, including 49.70% production, 13.23% transportation/material moving, 6.71% packaging-machine operators, and 1.14% inspectors.
sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 311800

sources.supports: Describes automatic tortilla forming, baking, cooling, stacking, sealing, vision-guided robotic handling, weight inspection, metal detection, boxing, and environmental monitoring.
sources.title: Integrated Flour Tortilla Production Line

sources.supports: States that GRUMA's US tortilla and corn-flour business serves industrial, retail, and foodservice customers and operates 20 tortilla and related-product plants.
sources.title: GRUMA Company Overview

sources.supports: Reports GRUMA USA volume down 2% to 388 thousand metric tons because of foodservice contraction and price sensitivity, cost-of-sales efficiencies, 20.6% full-year EBITDA margin, and strong better-for-you retail performance.
sources.title: GRUMA Fourth Quarter 2024 Results

sources.supports: Reports that 52.3% of US employer businesses have owners age 55 or older and that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer within five years.
sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan

sources.supports: Reports that more than 60% of owners in manufacturing, mining, and utilities are over 55 and describes the equipment, working-capital, buyer-pool, and transfer frictions of capital-intensive firms.
sources.title: Navigating the great small business ownership transition

sources.supports: Finds strong competition and switching among own-label food suppliers, transparent costs, competitive retailer pricing, and generally low own-label margins.
sources.title: Price inflation and competition in food and grocery manufacturing and supply

sources.supports: Requires covered facilities to maintain written hazard analyses and preventive controls, monitoring, corrections, verification, records, sanitation, allergen controls, and qualified staff.
sources.title: FSMA Final Rule for Preventive Controls for Human Food

sources.supports: Reports that the US Hispanic population grew 1.8% in 2023 to more than 65 million and accounted for just under 71% of total US population growth.
sources.title: New Estimates Highlight Differences in Growth Between the U.S. Hispanic and Non-Hispanic Populations

title: Tortilla Manufacturing
